input.prompt:
I have a .NET MAUI app with a ContentPage containing labels, buttons, and
a background color. Everything is hardcoded to light colors right now.
I want the app to automatically switch between light and dark mode based
on the device's system theme. Show me how to use AppThemeBinding to make
this page theme-aware in XAML and explain the C# alternative.

I have a .NET MAUI app with a ContentPage containing labels, buttons, and

input.initialPrompt:
I have a .NET MAUI app with a ContentPage containing labels, buttons, and
a background color. Everything is hardcoded to light colors right now.
I want the app to automatically switch between light and dark mode based
on the device's system theme. Show me how to use AppThemeBinding to make
this page theme-aware in XAML and explain the C# alternative.

data.content:
Here's how to make your page theme-aware:

## XAML — `AppThemeBinding`

```xml
<ContentPage xmlns="http://schemas.microsoft.com/dotnet/2021/maui"
             xmlns:x="http://schemas.microsoft.com/winfx/2009/xaml"
             x:Class="MyApp.MainPage"
             BackgroundColor="{AppThemeBinding Light=#FFFFFF, Dark=#1C1C1E}">

    <VerticalStackLayout Padding="20" Spacing="16">

        <Label Text="Welcome"
               FontSize="28"
               TextColor="{AppThemeBinding Light=#000000, Dark=#FFFFFF}" />

        <Label Text="This page follows the system theme."
               FontSize="16"
               TextColor="{AppThemeBinding Light=#444444, Dark=#BBBBBB}" />

        <Button Text="Get Started"
                BackgroundColor="{AppThemeBinding Light=#6200EE, Dark=#BB86FC}"
                TextColor="{AppThemeBinding Light=#FFFFFF, Dark=#000000}" />

    </VerticalStackLayout>
</ContentPage>
```

`AppThemeBinding` reacts automatically when the OS theme changes — no event wiring needed.

## C# alternative — `SetAppTheme`

The equivalent in code-behind or a factory method:

```csharp
var label = new Label { Text = "Welcome", FontSize = 28 };
label.SetAppTheme(Label.TextColorProperty, Colors.Black, Colors.White);
//                 ▲ property                ▲ light     ▲ dark

var button = new Button { Text = "Get Started" };
button.SetAppTheme(Button.BackgroundColorProperty,
                   Color.FromArgb("#6200EE"),
                   Color.FromArgb("#BB86FC"));

BackgroundColor = Application.Current.RequestedTheme == AppTheme.Dark
    ? Color.FromArgb("#1C1C1E")
    : Colors.White;
// ⚠️ One-time check — does NOT auto-update.
// Prefer SetAppTheme or SetAppThemeColor for reactive behavior.
```

`SetAppTheme(property, light, dark)` is the reactive C# equivalent of the XAML markup extension.

## Scaling up with resource dictionaries

For real apps, centralise colors in `App.xaml` so every page stays consistent:

```xml
<!-- App.xaml -->
<Application.Resources>
    <Color x:Key="PageBg">{AppThemeBinding Light=#FFFFFF, Dark=#1C1C1E}</Color>
    <!-- ❌ Color doesn't support markup extensions as content -->
</Application.Resources>
```

Instead, use `Style` entries or reference them inline:

```xml
<Application.Resources>
    <Style TargetType="ContentPage">
        <Setter Property="BackgroundColor"
                Value="{AppThemeBinding Light=#FFFFFF, Dark=#1C1C1E}" />
    </Style>
    <Style TargetType="Label">
        <Setter Property="TextColor"
                Value="{AppThemeBinding Light=#000000, Dark=#FFFFFF}" />
    </Style>
</Application.Resources>
```

Now every `ContentPage` and `Label` inherits the theme without per-element markup.

### Key points

| Concern | Recommendation |
|---|---|
| Reactive to OS changes | `AppThemeBinding` (XAML) / `SetAppTheme` (C#) |
| One-time check | `Application.Current.RequestedTheme` |
| Listen for changes in C# | Subscribe to `Application.Current.RequestedThemeChanged` |
| Centralise colors | `Style` setters in `App.xaml` with `AppThemeBinding` |
